food_subgroup: Braised offal and organ meat dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for braised pork intestine casserole; closest parent 'pork intestine food product' (FOODON_03302757) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Pig intestine) is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for the braised casserole preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for braised pork intestine casserole dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of pork intestine casserole is approximately 350 g, comprising braised intestines, tofu skin, mushrooms, and braising liquid served in a clay pot.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, large intestine, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 10397); adapted with braising-liquid and mixed-ingredient adjustment for casserole preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked pork large intestine (FDC ID 172543). Sodium adjusted upward to reflect soy-sauce-heavy braising liquid typical of Beijing-style preparation. Carbohydrate and sugar adjusted to account for rock sugar and soy sauce additions. Fat values reflect cooked intestine with rendered fat. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Secondary micronutrients retained from USDA SR Legacy where available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on braised mixed pork offal dish with soy sauce and minimal carbohydrate
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (University of Sydney GI Database contributing data)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork intestine casserole. GI estimated at 32 based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein and high-fat composition of the dish, analogous to other braised pork offal preparations with soy sauce. The dominant carbohydrate sources are rock sugar and soy sauce, both of which contribute modest glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 13.3) / 100 ≈ 4.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving ≈ 13.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-sauce-based braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100 g (≈2170 mg per 350 g serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but moderate fat and cholesterol content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycemic index (estimated 32) and low carbohydrate content make this dish relatively glycaemia-friendly for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat and cholesterol content warrant portion control and infrequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol, saturated fat, and sodium from pork intestine and braising liquid
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pork intestine casserole is high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 132 mg per 100 g), saturated fat (4.6 g per 100 g), and sodium (620 mg per 100 g), making it a high-concern food for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease; consumption should be infrequent and portions kept small.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Intestine Casserole (砂锅炖吊子, Shāguō dùn diào zi) is a traditional Beijing dish rooted in Manchu-Han imperial cuisine, slow-braised in a clay pot with soy sauce, Shaoxing rice wine, ginger, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, rock sugar, and dried chilli. The dish typically combines pork large and small intestines with tofu skin, wood ear mushrooms, and spring onion, all simmered until tender and deeply flavoured. The texture of the intestines is soft and gelatinous with a rich, savoury-sweet braising sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 14 g per 100 g) and fat (approximately 12.8 g per 100 g), with significant dietary cholesterol from the organ meat. Sodium content is elevated due to the soy-sauce-heavy braising liquid, making it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 32) owing to the minimal carbohydrate content, which is primarily derived from rock sugar and soy sauce. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI is favourable, but the high fat and cholesterol content require moderation. This dish is a non-halal, non-kosher, non-vegetarian preparation and contains soy and gluten allergens. It is served in Beijing-style Chinese restaurants and is emblematic of nose-to-tail cooking traditions in northern Chinese cuisine.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork intestine casserole?
Is pork intestine casserole high in cholesterol?
Can diabetics eat pork intestine casserole?
What does pork intestine casserole taste like?
What is Sha Guo Dun Diao Zi?
Is pork intestine casserole suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for pork intestine casserole exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked pork large intestine data with adjustments for braising liquid sodium, carbohydrate from rock sugar and soy sauce, and mixed-ingredient composition. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked pork intestine. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are derived from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profile for cooked pork large intestine and are approximate for the casserole preparation. Sodium is adjusted upward from the USDA SR Legacy base value (approximately 60 mg per 100 g for plain cooked intestine) to approximately 620 mg per 100 g to reflect the soy-sauce-heavy braising liquid; this is an estimation and actual sodium will vary by recipe and restaurant. GI is estimated at 32 based on analogous braised pork offal with soy sauce; no direct GI measurement for this dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. The dish is not halal as it contains pork intestine. Allergens include soy (soy sauce) and gluten (Shaoxing rice wine). This dish is served at See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore) at SGD 36.40 per portion as recorded in the field survey data.
